goal: Pin same-tree expansion + retroactive backfill — close the merge-commit + squash-content-commit coverage gap

summary.title: Pin: expand to same-tree neighbors + retroactive backfill for already-merged intents
summary.what: Pin no longer stops at the first tree_hash match. After it picks a primary commit, it expands to every other commit in the lookback window whose tree equals the primarys tree and writes the note to each (alreadyHasIntent keeps it idempotent). The same expansion now also runs for status=merged intents using StatusEvidence.MergedMainCommit as the primary, which retroactively backfills coverage on PRs merged before this fix landed. Bonus inline bug fix: replaced the early-continue on iv.Status != StatusProposed with a proposed-OR-merged guard so the new retroactive arm gets to run.
summary.why: GitHub merges (--no-ff or "Create a merge commit" style) produce a merge commit + squash content commit pair on main, both with identical trees. Pin used to write the note to whichever it hit first (the merge commit, since lookback iterates newest-first). The content commit underneath stayed uncovered, every PR. On this dogfood repo it accumulated to 11 uncovered out of 22 — every single PRs content commit. Coverage signal-to-noise was bad and would only get worse as the friend MVP rollout adds real PRs.
summary.user_goal: Coverage section in mainline status reflects intent reality, not GitHub merge mechanics. Sealed intents claim ALL the same-tree commits the merge produced.

decisions.point: Same-tree expansion vs explicit merge-commit detection
decisions.rationale: Tree-hash equality is the underlying invariant — a merge commit and the content commit it merged share a tree IFF the merge added no new content. Detecting "is this a merge commit" via parent count works for the canonical case but misses other valid same-tree pairs (rebases that produce duplicate trees, manual git replay, etc.). Tree equality covers the general class.

decisions.chose: integrate into the same Pin loop
decisions.rationale: Single code path means new and historical PRs use the same expansion. A separate "backfill" command would diverge over time. The retroactive arm is bounded by lookback so it does not scan the entire history every sync.

decisions.point: When to use which strategy label
decisions.chose: tree_hash for both proposed-fresh-pin and merged-retroactive
decisions.rationale: The semantic IS tree_hash — the secondary commit was identified as a same-tree neighbor of the primary, which is exactly what tree_hash means. Adding a new pin_backfill_neighbor strategy label would be more accurate but would balloon the strategy enum without operational value.

rejected.alternative: Detect merge commits explicitly via parent count
rejected.reason: Misses other same-tree cases (rebase replay, cherry-pick chains).

rejected.alternative: Walk back beyond the lookback window for retroactive backfill
rejected.reason: Would make every sync scan unbounded history. Lookback is the existing budget; retroactive uses it without expanding it.

rejected.alternative: Add a one-shot "pin backfill" CLI command for retroactive sweep
rejected.reason: New verb; the same logic running on every sync is cheaper to maintain. Sync converges over a few runs as the lookback window slides.

risks: On a long lookback with N intents and M same-tree-cluster sizes the inner loop is N*M*alreadyHasIntent. alreadyHasIntent does a NotesShow per call (one git fork via cat-file batch nowadays from PR #29). For lookback=50, intents=100, average-cluster=2, thats ~10k cat-file lookups per sync. Acceptable but not free; if status >200ms target slips on big repos this is a candidate to optimise via a notes-blob cache.

Tree equality across truly distinct PRs is theoretically possible (two PRs that produce byte-identical end states). Same-tree expansion would cross-pin them — both intents on both commits. This is actually what coverage wants (both intents IS the explanation), but the existing multi-intent-per-note infrastructure handles it cleanly.

Retroactive arm only fires when the historical pin commit is in the lookback. Older history beyond lookback stays as-was. Mitigated by the dogfood result (7 of 11 backfilled in one sweep on this repo); fully exhaustive backfill needs a one-shot scan that does not exist yet but is cheap to add if needed.
followups: For the 4 uncovered commits remaining on this repo, manually backfill via `mainline start --commits <sha>` — they have intent metadata quirks (sealed at non-final intermediate commits) that need a different fix.
Consider an unbounded retroactive backfill mode (`mainline pin --backfill-all` or via doctor) that walks the entire main history once. v0.4 if dogfood demands.
When the alreadyHasIntent N*M cost shows up in profiling, batch the notes-show calls via a single cat-file --batch session like collectAllEvents does.

architectural_claims: Pin now writes the same intent note to ALL same-tree commits in the lookback window, not just the first match.
Already-merged intents have their notes retroactively expanded to same-tree neighbors on every sync — backfills coverage as the lookback window naturally moves with new commits.
Pin loop iterates proposed AND merged intents (was proposed-only); merged intents only ever WRITE for retroactive backfill, never reset their merged status.
behavioral_changes: A new GitHub-style merge that produces both a merge commit and a squash content commit will now have BOTH commits covered after the next mainline sync.
Existing merged intents whose original pin landed only on the merge commit will retroactively get their content commit covered on the next sync (within the lookback window).
